flashcards.front: What is the primary use of the Student's t-distribution in statistical inference?
flashcards.back: It is used for estimating the mean of a normally distributed population when the sample size is small and the population standard deviation is unknown.

flashcards.front: How does the shape of the t-distribution compare to the standard normal distribution (z-distribution)?
flashcards.back: The t-distribution is similar in shape to the normal distribution (bell-shaped and symmetric) but has heavier tails. As the degrees of freedom increase, it converges to the standard normal distribution.

flashcards.front: What parameter defines the specific shape of a t-distribution and how is it calculated?
flashcards.back: The degrees of freedom (df). For a one-sample t-test, df = n - 1, where n is the sample size.

flashcards.front: What is the key difference between the formula for a z-statistic and a t-statistic?
flashcards.back: z = (x̄ - μ) / (σ/√n) uses the population standard deviation (σ). t = (x̄ - μ) / (s/√n) uses the sample standard deviation (s) as an estimate.

flashcards.front: When is the t-distribution essential for calculating confidence intervals?
flashcards.back: When constructing a confidence interval for a population mean and the population standard deviation is unknown, especially critical for small sample sizes (n < 30).

flashcards.front: What happens to the t-distribution as degrees of freedom approach infinity?
flashcards.back: It becomes identical to the standard normal distribution (z-distribution).
